metadata.title: SkyPilot Compute Offloading for VesselFM, SAM3, and Synthetic Generation
metadata.notes:
v1.2.0 (2026-03-12): USER DECISION — Oracle Cloud Always Free chosen for MLflow hosting. T1 status changed from NEEDS_USER_DECISION to DECIDED. T1b unblocked and now targets Oracle Cloud specifically (pulumi-oci provider). Added T1c for multi-arch Docker builds (aarch64 support). T2 updated with OCI env vars.
v1.1.0 (2026-03-12): Added Pulumi IaC integration for MLflow deployment. Pulumi integration is CONDITIONAL on user's IaC choice — if the user already has (or plans to have) a Pulumi subscription managing broader research infrastructure, Options C/D in T1 become the recommended MLflow deployment path because `pulumi up` automates the entire server provisioning. This repo is one part of a larger Pulumi-managed stack. The infra person is Pulumi-literate.

task.name: MLflow Remote Accessibility — Strategy Selection
task.description:
MLflow at http://minivess-mlflow:5000 is inside Docker Compose internal network. SkyPilot VMs cannot reach it. This was the #1 blocker for ALL remote training.
USER DECISION (2026-03-12): OPTION C — Oracle Cloud Always Free Tier chosen.
Rationale: Oracle Cloud Always Free provides 4 OCPU Arm Ampere A1, 24 GB RAM, 200 GB storage at $0/month forever. The MLflow server will be shared as a paper artifact, allowing reviewers and readers to browse experiments.
IMPORTANT: The Arm (aarch64) architecture requires either multi-arch Docker builds or `--platform linux/amd64` QEMU emulation. See T1c for multi-arch build support.
=== Other options evaluated (kept for reference) ===
OPTION A: DagsHub Managed MLflow (NOT CHOSEN) - Set MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=https://dagshub.com/USER/REPO.mlflow - Free tier: 20 GB storage, 100 experiments - Pro: $9/user/month for unlimited - Pros: zero infrastructure, always accessible, free for solo researcher - Cons: vendor dependency, data leaves local machine, 20 GB storage limit
OPTION B: Tailscale / Cloudflare Tunnel (NOT CHOSEN) - Cloudflare Tunnel: free, zero-trust, outbound-only connection CAVEAT: 100 MB upload limit — DEALBREAKER for ONNX models >100 MB - Tailscale: free for personal use (100 devices), no upload limit, WireGuard-based mesh VPN. - Pros: free, data stays local, no vendor lock-in - Cons: tunnel must run on dev machine during training, added latency
OPTION C: Oracle Cloud Always Free Tier — SELECTED - 4 OCPU ARM Ampere A1, 24 GB RAM, 200 GB block storage — $0/month forever - Deploy MLflow + PostgreSQL + MinIO on OCI ARM VM - Pulumi-oci provider automates provisioning via `pulumi up` - See T1b for the Pulumi stack implementation - See T1c for multi-arch Docker build support (aarch64) - Pros: truly free forever, always-on, no tunnel dependency, paper artifact - Cons: ARM compatibility testing required, OCI account setup (manual) - WITH PULUMI: setup complexity drops from HIGH to LOW (one-time stack dev)
OPTION D: Hetzner VPS (NOT CHOSEN — EUR 3.49/month CX22) - 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM, 40 GB — cheapest paid VPS option - Pros: cheap, x86 (no ARM issues), fast EU network - Cons: monthly cost when Oracle offers $0 forever
OPTION E: Post-hoc Sync (NOT CHOSEN) - SkyPilot VM trains with MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=mlruns (local filesystem) - Pros: zero infrastructure, works immediately - Cons: no real-time monitoring, no live metrics, not shareable as paper artifact

tdd.red: Write test in tests/v2/unit/test_mlflow_remote_accessibility.py: - test_mlflow_tracking_uri_remote_defined_in_env_example: Assert MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE exists in .env.example - test_resolve_tracking_uri_prefers_remote_when_set: When MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE is set and non-empty, resolve_tracking_uri() should return it (for SkyPilot context) - test_resolve_tracking_uri_falls_back_to_local: When MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE is unset, resolve_tracking_uri() returns the standard MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI value
tdd.green: 1. Add MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE to .env.example with documentation 2. Update resolve_tracking_uri() in src/minivess/observability/tracking.py to check MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE first when running in SkyPilot context (detected via SKYPILOT_TASK_ID env var)
tdd.verify: make test-staging — all new tests pass, no regressions uv run ruff check src/minivess/observability/tracking.py

task.name: Pulumi MLflow Stack — Oracle Cloud Always Free (OCI ARM A1.Flex)
task.description:
Oracle Cloud Always Free was chosen in T1 (DECIDED 2026-03-12). This task implements the Pulumi stack to deploy MLflow on OCI.
Prerequisites (manual, not automatable): - Create OCI account at https://cloud.oracle.com/ - Generate API signing key pair for Pulumi authentication - Note tenancy OCID, user OCID, compartment OCID, region
Create a Pulumi Python program (pulumi-oci provider) that deploys the complete MLflow tracking stack to Oracle Cloud Always Free:
1. Compute: OCI A1 Flex instance (4 OCPU Arm Ampere, 24 GB RAM) - Shape: VM.Standard.A1.Flex (Always Free eligible) - Boot volume: 50 GB (Always Free includes 200 GB total) - OS: Oracle Linux 9 or Ubuntu 22.04 (aarch64) 2. Networking: VCN + public subnet + security list - Ingress: allow HTTPS (443) and SSH (22) only - Egress: allow all (for Docker pulls, package installs) - MLflow behind nginx reverse proxy — port 5000 NOT exposed directly 3. DNS: A record via pulumi-cloudflare for `mlflow.minivess.fi` (or similar) 4. Docker: Install Docker + Docker Compose via cloud-init user data script 5. Services: Deploy MLflow + PostgreSQL + MinIO via Docker Compose (reuse existing deployment/docker-compose.yml with remote overrides) 6. TLS: Let's Encrypt via nginx reverse proxy (certbot or acme.sh) 7. Auth: Basic auth via nginx (htpasswd) for public access control
IMPORTANT: Arm (aarch64) architecture requires either multi-arch Docker builds or `--platform linux/amd64` QEMU emulation. See T1c for multi-arch build support. For the MLflow/PostgreSQL/MinIO stack, official images already support arm64 — no custom builds needed for server-side services.
Security hardening: - Firewall rules: only HTTPS (443) and SSH (22) inbound - MLflow behind nginx reverse proxy with Let's Encrypt TLS - PostgreSQL and MinIO on internal Docker network only (not exposed) - SSH key-only auth (no password), fail2ban for brute-force protection
The stack is designed to be part of a LARGER Pulumi-managed research infrastructure. This repo's stack is one component; the user's Pulumi org may manage other services (Langfuse, Prefect Cloud, etc.) alongside it.
Effort: 4h one-time stack development, then 5 min per deployment via `pulumi up`. Tear-down is `pulumi destroy`.
Provider: pulumi-oci (PyPI: pulumi-oci) — $0/month forever (Always Free Tier)

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_pulumi_mlflow_stack.py: - test_pulumi_project_yaml_exists: Assert deployment/pulumi/Pulumi.yaml exists and is valid YAML - test_pulumi_program_importable: Assert deployment/pulumi/__init__.py can be imported without error - test_pulumi_program_has_required_exports: Assert stack exports mlflow_url, postgres_connection, minio_endpoint - test_pulumi_dev_stack_config_exists: Assert deployment/pulumi/Pulumi.dev.yaml exists - test_pulumi_preview_succeeds: Run `pulumi preview --stack dev --non-interactive` in dry-run mode (requires pulumi CLI installed; skip with @pytest.mark.integration if not available) - test_pulumi_requirements_has_oci_provider: Assert deployment/pulumi/requirements.txt contains pulumi and pulumi-oci - test_pulumi_config_targets_oracle: Assert Pulumi.dev.yaml specifies Oracle Cloud (oci) as the provider - test_pulumi_program_provisions_a1_flex: Assert __init__.py references VM.Standard.A1.Flex shape - test_pulumi_program_has_vcn_and_security_list: Assert __init__.py creates VCN, subnet, and security list resources - test_pulumi_program_has_nginx_tls: Assert cloud-init or provisioner configures nginx with Let's Encrypt
tdd.green: 1. Create deployment/pulumi/Pulumi.yaml (project definition) 2. Create deployment/pulumi/__init__.py (Pulumi program): - Provision oci_core_vcn + oci_core_subnet + oci_core_security_list (ingress: 443 HTTPS + 22 SSH only) - Provision oci_core_instance (VM.Standard.A1.Flex, 4 OCPU, 24 GB RAM) - cloud-init user data: install Docker, Docker Compose, nginx, certbot - remote-exec provisioner: copy docker-compose.yml, nginx config, `docker compose up -d`, configure Let's Encrypt TLS - Export mlflow_url, postgres_connection, minio_endpoint 3. Create deployment/pulumi/Pulumi.dev.yaml (dev stack config): - oci_compartment_id: (must be set via `pulumi config set`) - oci_region: eu-frankfurt-1 (or user's home region) - instance_shape: VM.Standard.A1.Flex - instance_ocpus: 4 - instance_memory_gb: 24 - mlflow_domain: mlflow.minivess.fi - postgres_password: (secret, must be set via `pulumi config set --secret`) 4. Create deployment/pulumi/requirements.txt: - pulumi>=3.0 - pulumi-oci>=2.0 - pulumi-cloudflare>=5.0 (for DNS record) - pulumi-command>=0.9 (for remote-exec provisioning) 5. Create deployment/pulumi/cloud-init.yaml: - Install Docker CE (arm64), Docker Compose plugin - Install nginx, certbot, python3-certbot-nginx - Configure fail2ban for SSH brute-force protection
tdd.verify: - `pulumi preview --stack dev --cwd deployment/pulumi` succeeds (validates program syntax and config references — no actual deployment) - `pulumi up --stack dev --cwd deployment/pulumi` deploys MLflow (manual verification — MLflow UI accessible at https://mlflow.minivess.fi) - make test-staging — unit tests for file existence and structure pass - uv run ruff check deployment/pulumi/__init__.py

task.name: Multi-arch Docker base image builds (amd64 + arm64)
task.description:
The Oracle Cloud Always Free instance uses Arm Ampere A1 (aarch64), so Docker images need to be built for both amd64 and arm64 architectures. This affects Dockerfile.base and all flow Dockerfiles in deployment/docker/.
Two approaches (choose one): A. Multi-platform builds via `docker buildx` (RECOMMENDED) - Uses Docker Buildx with QEMU emulation for cross-compilation - Single build command produces images for both architectures - Images pushed to registry with multi-arch manifest - Slower build time (~2x) but simpler workflow
B. QEMU emulation only (`--platform linux/amd64`) - Run amd64 images on arm64 via QEMU user-space emulation - Simpler: no changes to Dockerfiles or build process - Slower runtime (~2-5x) due to instruction translation - Suitable as fallback if native arm64 builds are problematic
For the MLflow server stack (PostgreSQL, MinIO, MLflow, nginx), official images already support arm64 natively — no custom builds needed. Multi-arch builds are needed for the MinIVess flow images that run training, analysis, etc. on the Oracle instance (if needed) or on mixed-architecture compute clusters.
Changes: 1. Add `docker buildx create --use` setup to Makefile 2. Add `make docker-build-multiarch` target that builds all images for linux/amd64,linux/arm64 3. Update Dockerfile.base to use multi-stage build compatible with both architectures (avoid x86-specific instructions) 4. Test that resulting images work on both architectures

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_docker_multiarch.py: - test_makefile_has_multiarch_target: Assert Makefile contains "docker-build-multiarch" target - test_dockerfile_base_no_arch_specific_instructions: Parse Dockerfile.base, verify no hardcoded x86 package URLs or architecture-specific apt repo additions - test_docker_buildx_succeeds_for_base_image: Run `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64` for Dockerfile.base (requires Docker Buildx; skip with @pytest.mark.integration if not available)
tdd.green: 1. Add buildx builder setup and multi-arch build targets to Makefile 2. Audit Dockerfile.base for architecture-specific instructions 3. Verify official base images (nvidia/cuda, python) have arm64 variants NOTE: nvidia/cuda does NOT have arm64 — if CUDA is in base image, arm64 build uses python:3.12-slim instead (no GPU on Oracle ARM) 4. Add architecture detection in Dockerfile.base via TARGETARCH build arg
tdd.verify: - `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -f deployment/docker/Dockerfile.base .` succeeds for both architectures - Both architecture images produce working containers - make test-staging passes - Alternative: `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -f deployment/docker/Dockerfile.base .` succeeds (QEMU fallback)

task.name: Add MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE, OCI, and SKYPILOT env vars to .env.example
task.description:
Add all SkyPilot-related and Oracle Cloud environment variables to .env.example as the single source of truth (Rule #22). These variables are consumed by SkyPilot YAML task specs, by resolve_tracking_uri() when running on remote VMs, and by Pulumi for OCI provisioning.
Variables to add:
# Oracle Cloud MLflow hosting (T1 decision: Always Free tier) MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE=https://mlflow.minivess.fi OCI_TENANCY_OCID= (from OCI console) OCI_USER_OCID= (from OCI console) OCI_COMPARTMENT_OCID= (from OCI console) OCI_REGION=eu-frankfurt-1 OCI_FINGERPRINT= (API signing key fingerprint) OCI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH=~/.oci/oci_api_key.pem
# SkyPilot compute offloading SKYPILOT_CLOUD_CREDENTIALS= (path to cloud credential file) SKYPILOT_DEFAULT_CLOUD=runpod SKYPILOT_AUTOSTOP_MINUTES=30 SKYPILOT_API_SERVER_ENDPOINT= (optional, for shared state)
Also add a comment block noting the T1 decision (Oracle Cloud Always Free) and cross-referencing the FinOps report.

tdd.red: Write test in tests/v2/unit/test_env_single_source.py (extend existing): - test_env_example_has_skypilot_section: Assert .env.example contains MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE - test_env_example_has_skypilot_default_cloud: Assert .env.example contains SKYPILOT_DEFAULT_CLOUD - test_env_example_has_skypilot_autostop: Assert .env.example contains SKYPILOT_AUTOSTOP_MINUTES
tdd.green: Add the SkyPilot section to .env.example between the HPO section and the Prefect logging section.
tdd.verify: make test-staging Verify .env.example parses correctly (no syntax errors)

task.name: Extract train_fold as standalone callable module
task.description:
The train_one_fold_task() in train_flow.py is a Prefect @task. SkyPilot VMs run training without Prefect (report Section 18, Q5: "No Prefect on VM"). Extract the core fold training logic into a standalone module that can be called both: 1. By the Prefect @task (existing path — no behavior change) 2. By a __main__ entry point for SkyPilot VMs
Create src/minivess/pipeline/train_fold.py containing: - train_single_fold() — pure function, no Prefect decorators - __main__ block that parses EXPERIMENT env var + Hydra overrides - MLflow logging via ExperimentTracker (same as current train_flow.py) - SKYPILOT_TASK_ID tag for grouping runs across preemption recoveries
The existing train_one_fold_task() in train_flow.py becomes a thin wrapper that calls train_single_fold().
IMPORTANT: This does NOT violate Rule #17 (no standalone scripts). The SkyPilot VM is a managed compute environment with proper lifecycle. The Prefect flow still orchestrates locally. See report Section 18, Q5.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_train_fold_module.py: - test_train_fold_module_importable: from minivess.pipeline.train_fold import train_single_fold - test_train_single_fold_signature: Verify train_single_fold accepts (fold_id, fold_split, config, checkpoint_dir) - test_train_single_fold_returns_dict: Mock model/data, verify return type is dict with expected keys - test_train_fold_has_main_entry_point: Verify __main__ block exists (ast.parse, check for if __name__) - test_train_fold_logs_skypilot_task_id: When SKYPILOT_TASK_ID env var is set, verify it appears in MLflow tags - test_train_one_fold_task_delegates_to_train_single_fold: Verify train_one_fold_task calls train_single_fold internally
tdd.green: 1. Create src/minivess/pipeline/train_fold.py with train_single_fold() 2. Refactor train_one_fold_task() to delegate to train_single_fold() 3. Add __main__ block with EXPERIMENT env var parsing 4. Add SKYPILOT_TASK_ID tagging in MLflow run context
tdd.verify: make test-staging — new tests pass uv run ruff check src/minivess/pipeline/train_fold.py uv run mypy src/minivess/pipeline/train_fold.py Verify existing train_flow tests still pass (no behavior change)

task.name: Add compute_backend config parameter to training flow
task.description:
The training_flow() currently accepts compute="auto" but ignores it (report Section 3: "compute parameter accepted, never used"). Replace the ignored `compute` parameter with a proper `compute_backend` parameter that dispatches between local Docker execution and SkyPilot.
Valid values: - "local_docker" (default) — existing path, no behavior change - "skypilot" — launches training on SkyPilot VM, polls until complete
Add ComputeBackend enum to src/minivess/config/models.py. Update training_flow() signature: compute_backend: str = "local_docker". The actual SkyPilot dispatch logic is implemented in T5.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_compute_backend.py: - test_compute_backend_enum_values: ComputeBackend has LOCAL_DOCKER and SKYPILOT members - test_training_flow_accepts_compute_backend_param: Inspect training_flow signature, verify compute_backend parameter exists - test_training_flow_default_compute_backend_is_local_docker: Default value is "local_docker" - test_compute_backend_in_config_dict: When config_dict has compute_backend key, it overrides the parameter - test_invalid_compute_backend_raises: ComputeBackend("invalid") raises ValueError
tdd.green: 1. Add ComputeBackend enum to src/minivess/config/models.py 2. Update training_flow() signature: replace compute with compute_backend 3. Add compute_backend extraction from config_dict 4. Add validation: ComputeBackend(compute_backend) early in flow
tdd.verify: make test-staging — new and existing tests pass uv run ruff check src/minivess/config/models.py uv run ruff check src/minivess/orchestration/flows/train_flow.py

task.name: Implement SkyPilot dispatch path in training flow
task.description:
When compute_backend="skypilot", the training flow must: 1. Build the experiment config (Hydra-zen) 2. Call SkyPilotLauncher.launch_training_job() with env overrides 3. Poll via wait_sky_job() until SUCCEEDED/FAILED 4. Read results from MLflow (training logged by remote VM) 5. Return TrainingFlowResult with source="skypilot"
The local Prefect flow owns the lifecycle. The SkyPilot VM is a "dumb" training worker that logs to MLflow only (report Section 18, Q2).
For multi-fold training on SkyPilot: launch one job per fold (parallel) using sky.jobs.launch --async, then barrier-wait for all.
Update SkyPilotLauncher to: - Accept MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE from env - Pass all training config as env vars to the SkyPilot task - Support --async mode for parallel fold launches - Return structured result with job_id, status, cloud, cost_estimate

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_dispatch.py: - test_skypilot_dispatch_calls_launcher: Mock SkyPilotLauncher, verify launch_training_job called when compute_backend="skypilot" - test_skypilot_dispatch_passes_config_as_envs: Verify loss_name, model_family, max_epochs passed as env overrides - test_skypilot_dispatch_passes_mlflow_remote_uri: Verify MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI set to MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE value - test_skypilot_dispatch_waits_for_completion: Mock wait_sky_job, verify it is called with correct job_id - test_skypilot_dispatch_raises_on_failure: When job status is FAILED, RuntimeError is raised - test_skypilot_dispatch_returns_correct_result: TrainingFlowResult has source="skypilot" - test_skypilot_dispatch_parallel_folds: For num_folds=3, verify 3 jobs launched with --async - test_skypilot_dispatch_dry_run_no_launch: When SkyPilot not installed, dry_run result returned
tdd.green: 1. Add _dispatch_skypilot() private function in train_flow.py 2. Update training_flow() to call _dispatch_skypilot() when compute_backend == "skypilot" 3. Update SkyPilotLauncher.launch_training_job() to accept mlflow_uri and async_mode parameters 4. Add SkyPilotLauncher.wait_all_jobs() for barrier pattern
tdd.verify: make test-staging — all dispatch tests pass uv run ruff check src/minivess/compute/skypilot_launcher.py uv run ruff check src/minivess/orchestration/flows/train_flow.py

task.name: Update train_generic.yaml with MOUNT_CACHED, job_recovery, secrets
task.description:
The existing train_generic.yaml (deployment/skypilot/train_generic.yaml) has several gaps identified in the report: 1. Uses MOUNT (slow) instead of MOUNT_CACHED (9x faster writes) 2. No job_recovery section for spot preemption handling 3. No secrets section for HF_TOKEN 4. MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI uses hardcoded pattern instead of MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE 5. run section invokes prefect deployment run — should use python -m minivess.pipeline.train_fold (no Prefect on VM) 6. Missing RunPod in failover chain (should be first, cheapest) 7. setup section uses pip instead of uv
Also update train_hpo_sweep.yaml with same fixes.
Update the failover chain to prioritize consumer GPUs: RunPod RTX4090 ($0.34) -> RunPod RTX3090 ($0.22) -> RunPod A100 ($1.74) -> Lambda A100 ($0.78) -> GCP Spot A100

tdd.red: Update tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_configs.py: - test_train_generic_uses_mount_cached: Assert /checkpoints mount mode is MOUNT_CACHED, not MOUNT - test_train_generic_has_job_recovery: Assert job_recovery section exists in resources - test_train_generic_has_hf_token_secret: Assert secrets section contains HF_TOKEN - test_train_generic_uses_remote_mlflow_uri: Assert MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI references MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE - test_train_generic_uses_train_fold_module: Assert run section contains "python -m minivess.pipeline.train_fold" (NOT "prefect deployment run") - test_train_generic_setup_uses_uv: Assert setup section contains "uv sync" (not "pip install") - test_train_generic_runpod_in_failover: Assert any_of contains a RunPod entry - test_train_hpo_uses_mount_cached: Same MOUNT_CACHED check for HPO YAML
tdd.green: 1. Rewrite deployment/skypilot/train_generic.yaml with all fixes 2. Rewrite deployment/skypilot/train_hpo_sweep.yaml with same fixes 3. Update consumer GPU failover chain per report Section 7
tdd.verify: make test-staging — all YAML config tests pass sky check --dry-run (if SkyPilot installed)

task.name: Add skypilot extras group to pyproject.toml
task.description:
SkyPilot is not currently in pyproject.toml. Add it as an optional extras group so it can be installed with uv sync --extra sky.
Package: skypilot-nightly[runpod,aws,gcp,kubernetes] (nightly required for RunPod support — not available in stable release)
Also add runpod>=1.6 as a dependency of the sky extras group.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_extras.py: - test_pyproject_has_sky_extras_group: Parse pyproject.toml, assert optional-dependencies has "sky" key - test_sky_extras_includes_skypilot: Assert "skypilot-nightly" in sky extras dependencies - test_sky_extras_includes_runpod: Assert "runpod" in sky extras dependencies
tdd.green: 1. Add [project.optional-dependencies.sky] to pyproject.toml 2. Include skypilot-nightly[runpod,aws,gcp,kubernetes] and runpod>=1.6
tdd.verify: uv sync --extra sky (should resolve without errors) make test-staging

task.name: HPO flow pre-generates trial grid from Optuna search space
task.description:
For SkyPilot HPO, use the pre-generated grid approach (report Section 12, Option 1 — recommended for MVP). Optuna's TPE sampler generates trial configs locally, then all trials are launched as independent SkyPilot jobs. No shared Optuna database needed on remote VMs.
Add generate_trial_grid() to src/minivess/optimization/hpo_engine.py: - Takes HPOEngine + search_space + n_trials - Calls study.ask() n_trials times - Returns list of flat config dicts (each is a complete training config) - Each config gets a unique trial_tag for MLflow grouping
Update hpo_flow.py to use generate_trial_grid() when compute_backend="skypilot", then launch all trials in parallel.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_hpo_trial_grid.py: - test_generate_trial_grid_returns_list_of_dicts: generate_trial_grid(engine, search_space, n_trials=4) returns list of 4 dicts - test_generate_trial_grid_dicts_have_required_keys: Each dict has learning_rate, batch_size, loss_name keys - test_generate_trial_grid_dicts_have_trial_tag: Each dict has unique trial_tag string - test_generate_trial_grid_values_in_search_space: learning_rate is between 1e-5 and 1e-2, batch_size in [1,2,4] - test_generate_trial_grid_reproducible_with_seed: Same seed produces same grid - test_generate_trial_grid_merges_base_config: Base config keys (model_family, max_epochs) present in output
tdd.green: 1. Add generate_trial_grid() to src/minivess/optimization/hpo_engine.py 2. Uses HPOEngine.study.ask() + suggest_params() pattern 3. Returns list of flat training config dicts
tdd.verify: make test-staging — grid generation tests pass uv run ruff check src/minivess/optimization/hpo_engine.py

task.name: HPO flow dispatches trials as parallel SkyPilot async jobs
task.description:
Update hpo_flow.py to support compute_backend="skypilot": 1. Generate trial grid via generate_trial_grid() (T8) 2. For each trial config, call SkyPilotLauncher.launch_training_job() with async_mode=True (equivalent to sky jobs launch --async) 3. Collect all job_ids 4. Return job_ids for the barrier task (T10)
The SkyPilot launch is non-blocking — each trial runs on its own VM. Parallelism is limited only by cloud GPU availability.
Add compute_backend parameter to hpo_flow() signature. When compute_backend="local_docker", use existing sequential path. When compute_backend="skypilot", use parallel async launches.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_hpo_skypilot_dispatch.py: - test_hpo_flow_accepts_compute_backend_param: Inspect hpo_flow signature - test_hpo_skypilot_launches_n_async_jobs: Mock SkyPilotLauncher, verify launch called n_trials times with async - test_hpo_skypilot_passes_trial_configs_as_envs: Each launch call has different learning_rate, batch_size - test_hpo_skypilot_returns_job_ids: Result dict includes job_ids list - test_hpo_local_docker_uses_existing_path: When compute_backend="local_docker", run_deployment called (not SkyPilot)
tdd.green: 1. Add compute_backend parameter to hpo_flow() 2. Add _dispatch_hpo_skypilot() private function 3. Use generate_trial_grid() + SkyPilotLauncher.launch_training_job(async_mode=True) 4. Return result dict with job_ids for barrier
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/orchestration/flows/hpo_flow.py

task.name: Completion barrier — poll all job_ids, trigger Analysis Flow
task.description:
After launching N parallel SkyPilot HPO trials, the HPO flow must wait for ALL trials to reach a terminal state (SUCCEEDED, FAILED, CANCELLED) before returning. This is the "barrier" pattern (report Section 13).
Add wait_all_sky_jobs() to src/minivess/compute/prefect_sky_tasks.py: - Takes list of job_ids - Polls each using SkyPilotLauncher.get_job_status() - Returns summary: {succeeded: N, failed: N, cancelled: N, total: N} - Logs progress every poll interval - Configurable poll_interval (default 60s) and max_wait (default 48h) - On timeout: log warning, return partial results (don't raise)
The HPO flow calls wait_all_sky_jobs() after launching, then returns. The PipelineTriggerChain (T11) handles downstream flow dispatch.
Key design: Analysis Flow does NOT need to know about HPO. It queries MLflow for all FINISHED runs in the experiment and builds ensembles from whatever it finds (report Section 13).

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_sky_completion_barrier.py: - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_returns_summary: Mock get_job_status returning SUCCEEDED for all, verify summary - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_handles_mixed_results: 2 SUCCEEDED + 1 FAILED, verify summary counts - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_polls_until_terminal: Mock status progression: PENDING -> RUNNING -> SUCCEEDED - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_timeout_returns_partial: When max_wait exceeded, returns partial results without raising - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_empty_list: Empty job_ids list returns {succeeded:0, failed:0, total:0} - test_wait_all_sky_jobs_logs_progress: Verify logging output includes progress messages
tdd.green: 1. Add wait_all_sky_jobs() to src/minivess/compute/prefect_sky_tasks.py 2. Wraps individual wait_sky_job() calls with summary aggregation 3. Add @task decorator for Prefect visibility
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/compute/prefect_sky_tasks.py

task.name: Update PipelineTriggerChain for HPO barrier and Analysis dispatch
task.description:
The PipelineTriggerChain currently runs flows sequentially: acquisition -> data -> train -> post_training -> analyze -> ...
For HPO workflows, the chain should be: data -> hpo_flow (contains training + barrier) -> analyze -> deploy -> dashboard
The HPO flow acts as the barrier point. When it returns, ALL trials are complete, and Analysis Flow can safely query MLflow for all runs.
Changes: 1. Add "hpo" to _DEFAULT_FLOWS (between "train" and "post_training") 2. Make "train" and "hpo" mutually exclusive (register one or the other) 3. Add register_hpo_flow() convenience method 4. When HPO flow is registered, skip "train" flow automatically
Also update trigger.py to pass compute_backend to registered flows.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_trigger_chain_hpo.py: - test_trigger_chain_has_hpo_slot: PipelineTriggerChain().flow_names contains "hpo" - test_hpo_replaces_train_in_chain: When hpo registered and train not, hpo runs and train is skipped - test_hpo_before_analyze_in_order: "hpo" comes before "analyze" in flow_names - test_chain_passes_compute_backend: When compute_backend="skypilot", it is passed to registered flow - test_chain_result_includes_hpo: After chain run, results include hpo flow entry
tdd.green: 1. Add "hpo" to _DEFAULT_FLOWS after "train" 2. Update run_chain() to handle hpo/train mutual exclusion 3. Pass compute_backend in trigger_source kwargs
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/orchestration/trigger.py

task.name: SkyPilot cost tracking — log $/job as MLflow metrics
task.description:
Every SkyPilot job has a cost (cloud provider charges). Track this as MLflow metrics for FinOps visibility: - sky_cost_total_usd: total cost of the SkyPilot job - sky_cost_per_hour_usd: hourly rate charged - sky_duration_hours: total runtime - sky_cloud_provider: which cloud the job ran on - sky_gpu_type: GPU type used - sky_spot_instance: whether spot was used
SkyPilot provides cost info via sky.jobs.queue() and sky.core.cost_report(). Extract after job completion in wait_sky_job() and log to MLflow.
These metrics feed into the Dashboard Flow's cost section (T13).

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_cost_tracking.py: - test_cost_tracking_extracts_from_sky_status: Mock sky.jobs.queue() response, verify cost fields extracted - test_cost_tracking_logs_to_mlflow: Verify mlflow.log_metric called with sky_cost_total_usd - test_cost_tracking_handles_missing_cost: When cost info unavailable, logs NaN (not error) - test_cost_tracking_includes_gpu_type: sky_gpu_type tag set correctly
tdd.green: 1. Add _extract_sky_cost() to src/minivess/compute/skypilot_launcher.py 2. Add log_sky_cost_to_mlflow() utility function 3. Call from wait_sky_job() after job reaches terminal state
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/compute/skypilot_launcher.py

task.name: Dashboard Flow cost section — aggregate SkyPilot spend
task.description:
Add a cost aggregation adapter to the Dashboard Flow that reads sky_cost_total_usd metrics from all MLflow runs in an experiment and generates: - Total spend per experiment (table) - Cost per trial in HPO sweep (bar chart) - Cost breakdown by cloud provider and GPU type - Cumulative spend timeline
The adapter reads from MLflow (DuckDB analytics) and writes cost_summary.json + cost_breakdown.png to the dashboard output dir.
This is a best-effort addition to the existing dashboard flow — if no sky_cost metrics exist, the section is silently skipped.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_dashboard_cost_section.py: - test_cost_adapter_reads_sky_metrics_from_mlflow: Mock MLflow client, verify sky_cost_total_usd queried - test_cost_adapter_generates_summary_json: Verify cost_summary.json written with total_usd, per_trial breakdown - test_cost_adapter_skips_when_no_sky_metrics: When no runs have sky_cost metrics, adapter returns empty dict - test_cost_adapter_aggregates_by_provider: Multiple runs from different clouds, verify per-provider totals
tdd.green: 1. Create src/minivess/pipeline/dashboard_adapters/cost_adapter.py 2. Add CostSummaryAdapter class with generate() method 3. Register in dashboard_flow.py adapter list
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/pipeline/dashboard_adapters/cost_adapter.py

task.name: SAM3 fine-tuning SkyPilot YAML (RTX 4090/5090/A100, 48h budget)
task.description:
Create deployment/skypilot/train_sam3.yaml for SAM3 model fine-tuning on cloud GPUs. SAM3 has specific requirements: - 848M params, ViT-32L encoder, SDPA mandatory - sam3_vanilla: 2.9 GB — fits RTX 3090+ trivially - sam3_hybrid: 7.5 GB — fits RTX 4090 (24 GB) - Full fine-tuning: needs >= 32 GB VRAM (RTX 5090 or A100) - LoRA/QLoRA fine-tuning: fits RTX 4090 (24 GB) - HF_TOKEN required for gated model download
Failover chain (SAM3 specific): RTX 4090 ($0.34/hr) for LoRA -> RTX 5090 ($0.69/hr) for full fine-tune -> A100 ($1.74/hr) for large batch -> H100 ($1.99/hr) for speed
Budget guard: autostop after 48h max to prevent runaway costs. Estimated cost: $12-50 depending on GPU and fine-tuning strategy.

tdd.red: Add to tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_configs.py: - test_train_sam3_yaml_exists: deployment/skypilot/train_sam3.yaml exists - test_train_sam3_yaml_valid: Parses without error - test_train_sam3_has_hf_token_secret: secrets section contains HF_TOKEN - test_train_sam3_has_job_recovery: job_recovery section exists - test_train_sam3_uses_mount_cached: /checkpoints mount mode is MOUNT_CACHED - test_train_sam3_has_sdpa_env: envs contain ATTN_IMPLEMENTATION=sdpa - test_train_sam3_has_autostop: resources section has idle_minutes_to_autostop - test_train_sam3_model_family_is_sam3: MODEL_FAMILY env is sam3_vanilla or contains sam3
tdd.green: Create deployment/skypilot/train_sam3.yaml with: - Consumer GPU failover chain - HF_TOKEN secret - MOUNT_CACHED checkpoints - job_recovery: FAILOVER + max_restarts - SDPA mandatory env var - 48h autostop budget guard - python -m minivess.pipeline.train_fold entry point
tdd.verify: make test-staging — SAM3 YAML tests pass

task.name: VesselFM integration SkyPilot YAML
task.description:
Create deployment/skypilot/train_vesselfm.yaml for VesselFM foundation model fine-tuning. VesselFM is a vessel-specific foundation model that requires: - HF_TOKEN for gated model access - Large VRAM (estimated 16-48 GB depending on variant) - Long training time (estimated 24-72h for fine-tuning)
The YAML should support both: 1. Feature extraction (frozen backbone, train head only) — faster, less VRAM 2. Full fine-tuning — slow, needs large VRAM
Failover chain: RTX 5090 ($0.69/hr, 32 GB) for feature extraction -> A100 ($1.74/hr, 80 GB) for full fine-tuning -> H100 ($1.99/hr, 80 GB) for speed
NOTE: VesselFM ModelAdapter must exist before this YAML can be used. This task creates the SkyPilot YAML only — the adapter is a separate task.

tdd.red: Add to tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_configs.py: - test_train_vesselfm_yaml_exists: deployment/skypilot/train_vesselfm.yaml exists - test_train_vesselfm_yaml_valid: Parses without error - test_train_vesselfm_has_hf_token_secret: secrets section contains HF_TOKEN - test_train_vesselfm_has_large_vram_gpus: Failover chain includes A100-80GB or RTX5090 - test_train_vesselfm_uses_mount_cached: /checkpoints mount mode is MOUNT_CACHED - test_train_vesselfm_model_family: MODEL_FAMILY env contains "vesselfm"
tdd.green: Create deployment/skypilot/train_vesselfm.yaml
tdd.verify: make test-staging

task.name: Synthetic generator training SkyPilot YAMLs (VesselVAE, VQ-VAE)
task.description:
Create SkyPilot YAMLs for training synthetic vascular generators:
1. deployment/skypilot/train_vessel_vae.yaml - VesselVAE: lightweight VAE for synthetic vessel generation - ~4 GB VRAM, fits RTX 3090 easily - Estimated 6h training, cost ~$1.32 on RTX 3090 - Failover: RTX3090 ($0.22) -> RTX4090 ($0.34) -> RTX5090 ($0.69)
2. deployment/skypilot/train_vqvae.yaml - VQ-VAE: vector-quantized VAE for patch-based generation - ~4-8 GB VRAM - Estimated 8h training, cost ~$1.76 on RTX 3090 - Same failover chain as VesselVAE
Both generators produce synthetic training data that feeds into the data flow for augmentation and drift detection experiments.
NOTE: Generator model implementations are separate tasks. These YAMLs only define the SkyPilot compute configuration.

tdd.red: Add to tests/v2/unit/test_skypilot_configs.py: - test_train_vessel_vae_yaml_exists: deployment/skypilot/train_vessel_vae.yaml exists - test_train_vessel_vae_yaml_valid: Parses without error - test_train_vessel_vae_uses_consumer_gpu: First failover entry is RTX3090 or RTX4090 - test_train_vqvae_yaml_exists: deployment/skypilot/train_vqvae.yaml exists - test_train_vqvae_yaml_valid: Parses without error - test_generator_yamls_use_mount_cached: Both YAMLs use MOUNT_CACHED for checkpoints
tdd.green: 1. Create deployment/skypilot/train_vessel_vae.yaml 2. Create deployment/skypilot/train_vqvae.yaml 3. Consumer GPU failover chains optimized for lightweight models
tdd.verify: make test-staging

task.name: Checkpoint-on-preemption logic for spot instance training
task.description:
SkyPilot spot instances get a 5-second SIGTERM before SIGKILL on preemption (RunPod Community Cloud). The training loop must handle this gracefully:
1. Register a SIGTERM handler in train_single_fold() that: - Sets a "preemption_requested" flag - Saves current model/optimizer state to /checkpoints immediately - Logs "preempted_at_epoch" to MLflow
2. On restart (SkyPilot job_recovery: FAILOVER), the training loop: - Checks for existing checkpoint in /checkpoints (MOUNT_CACHED = persistent) - Loads model/optimizer/scheduler/RNG state - Resumes from last saved epoch - Logs "resumed_from_epoch" to MLflow - Uses SKYPILOT_TASK_ID to group preemption/recovery runs
3. The existing SegmentationTrainer already saves epoch_latest.yaml and periodic checkpoints. Extend it with: - save_on_signal() method (5-second budget checkpoint) - resume_from_checkpoint() method (load state dict + epoch) - SIGTERM signal handler registration
This is critical for cost optimization: spot instances are 3-6x cheaper but can be preempted at any time.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_spot_checkpointing.py: - test_trainer_registers_sigterm_handler: When running with SKYPILOT_TASK_ID set, SIGTERM handler registered - test_save_on_signal_creates_checkpoint: Calling save_on_signal() creates a checkpoint file - test_save_on_signal_within_5_seconds: save_on_signal() completes within 5 seconds (timeout test) - test_resume_from_checkpoint_loads_state: When checkpoint exists, training resumes from saved epoch - test_resume_from_checkpoint_logs_to_mlflow: resumed_from_epoch metric logged after resumption - test_no_checkpoint_starts_fresh: When no checkpoint exists, training starts from epoch 0 - test_skypilot_task_id_groups_runs: Multiple runs with same SKYPILOT_TASK_ID share the group tag
tdd.green: 1. Add save_on_signal() to SegmentationTrainer 2. Add resume_from_checkpoint() to SegmentationTrainer 3. Register SIGTERM handler when SKYPILOT_TASK_ID is set 4. Add SKYPILOT_TASK_ID tagging in MLflow
tdd.verify: make test-staging uv run ruff check src/minivess/pipeline/trainer.py

task.name: SkyPilot work pool in Prefect deployments
task.description:
Add a "sky-gpu-pool" work pool configuration to Prefect deployments so that flows can be routed to SkyPilot compute via Prefect's work pool system. This enables: - Prefect UI visibility of SkyPilot-dispatched work - Work pool routing: cpu-pool for orchestration, gpu-pool for local training, sky-gpu-pool for cloud training - Future: Prefect workers that natively dispatch to SkyPilot
Update src/minivess/orchestration/deployments.py: - Add SKY_GPU_POOL constant - Add sky-gpu-pool to deployment configs - Update training-flow deployment to accept work_pool parameter
This is a "nice to have" for better Prefect UI integration. The core SkyPilot dispatch (T5) works without this.

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/unit/test_sky_work_pool.py: - test_deployments_has_sky_gpu_pool: SKY_GPU_POOL constant defined in deployments.py - test_sky_gpu_pool_value: SKY_GPU_POOL == "sky-gpu-pool" - test_training_deployment_supports_sky_pool: Training flow deployment config accepts work_pool param
tdd.green: 1. Add SKY_GPU_POOL = "sky-gpu-pool" to deployments.py 2. Update training deployment to include sky-gpu-pool option
tdd.verify: make test-staging

task.name: Update deployment/CLAUDE.md with SkyPilot operational guide
task.description:
Add a SkyPilot section to deployment/CLAUDE.md documenting: 1. How to set up SkyPilot (uv sync --extra sky, cloud credentials) 2. How to configure MLflow accessibility (ref T1 decision) 3. How to launch a single training job via SkyPilot 4. How to run an HPO sweep on cloud GPUs 5. How to monitor running jobs (sky jobs queue, sky jobs logs) 6. Cost optimization tips (consumer GPUs, spot, autostop) 7. Troubleshooting: common failure modes and fixes 8. Environment variables reference (MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE, etc.)
Also update the "Running Flows" section to include SkyPilot paths: docker compose run train (local) vs compute_backend=skypilot (cloud)
IMPORTANT: Do NOT create a new README or docs file. Update the existing deployment/CLAUDE.md which is the canonical deployment reference.

tdd.red: This is a documentation task. Tests verify the docs are accurate: - test_deployment_claude_md_has_skypilot_section: deployment/CLAUDE.md contains "## SkyPilot" section header - test_deployment_claude_md_references_env_vars: deployment/CLAUDE.md mentions MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE - test_deployment_claude_md_references_train_fold: deployment/CLAUDE.md mentions "python -m minivess.pipeline.train_fold"
tdd.green: Update deployment/CLAUDE.md with SkyPilot operational guide section.
tdd.verify: make test-staging Read deployment/CLAUDE.md and verify accuracy against implementation

task.name: Integration test: DynUNet training on RunPod via SkyPilot (dry run)
task.description:
Create an integration test that verifies the full SkyPilot dispatch path works end-to-end in dry-run mode (no actual cloud launch):
1. Build experiment config via Hydra-zen 2. Set compute_backend="skypilot" 3. SkyPilotLauncher runs in dry-run mode (no sky import needed) 4. Verify config is correctly translated to SkyPilot env vars 5. Verify MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE is passed correctly 6. Verify wait_sky_job would be called with correct job_id 7. Verify TrainingFlowResult has source="skypilot"
This test runs in the staging tier (no GPU, no cloud, no Docker). It validates the wiring between all Phase 1-2 components.
A separate manual test (not automated) verifies actual RunPod execution: MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE=https://... \ sky jobs launch deployment/skypilot/train_generic.yaml \ --env LOSS_NAME=dice_ce --env MODEL_FAMILY=dynunet --env MAX_EPOCHS=2

tdd.red: Create tests/v2/integration/test_skypilot_e2e_dry_run.py: - test_full_skypilot_dispatch_dry_run: Set compute_backend="skypilot", verify full dispatch path - test_skypilot_config_translation: Hydra config -> SkyPilot env vars mapping is correct - test_skypilot_mlflow_uri_propagation: MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI_REMOTE appears in launch config - test_skypilot_hpo_dispatch_dry_run: HPO flow with n_trials=3, compute_backend="skypilot" in dry run
tdd.green: Implement integration tests using monkeypatch for env vars and SkyPilot dry-run mode.
tdd.verify: make test-staging (integration tests included in staging if no Docker needed) Verify all 4 tests pass

step.note: DECIDED (2026-03-12): Oracle Cloud Always Free chosen. No further user input needed.
Pulumi OCI stack + multi-arch Docker builds. Both unblocked by T1 decision. Can run in parallel.
Independent tasks: env vars (now includes OCI vars), pyproject.toml, train_fold extraction, HPO grid — all can run in parallel
SkyPilot dispatch + YAML updates — can run in parallel
HPO dispatch + barrier — depend on T5
Dashboard cost section — depends on T12
SAM3, VesselFM, synthetic generators — all independent, all depend on T6
Docs + dry-run integration test — after all implementation

item.reason: PROMOTED to T1b (2026-03-12). User chose Oracle Cloud Always Free (Option C). T1b is now NOT_STARTED with P1 priority. T1c added for multi-arch Docker builds.

deferred.item: Cloud-deploy MLflow + PostgreSQL via Pulumi — PROMOTED to T1b + T1c (Oracle Cloud Always Free)
Shared Optuna study on cloud PostgreSQL
SkyPilot API server for shared state
Prefect on SkyPilot VMs (Prefect Cloud integration)

risk.description: Oracle Cloud Always Free instance provisioning delays (OCI account creation, ARM compatibility issues)
risk.mitigation: DECIDED (2026-03-12): Oracle Cloud Always Free chosen. Risk reduced from HIGH to MEDIUM. Pulumi stack (T1b) automates provisioning. Fallback: use Option E (post-hoc sync) during OCI setup. Multi-arch Docker builds (T1c) address ARM compatibility.

risk.description: RunPod GPU availability varies — 151+ outages in 6 months
risk.mitigation: SkyPilot multi-cloud failover: RunPod -> Lambda -> GCP Spot -> AWS. Never depend on single provider.

risk.description: Spot preemption during SAM3 fine-tuning (48h job) loses progress
risk.mitigation: MOUNT_CACHED checkpoints (T6) + SIGTERM handler (T17) + job_recovery: FAILOVER. Checkpoint interval: every 5 epochs.

risk.description: SkyPilot nightly package instability (RunPod support not in stable)
risk.mitigation: Pin skypilot-nightly version in pyproject.toml. Test before upgrading. Fall back to Lambda/GCP if RunPod support breaks.

risk.description: Cloud GPU costs exceed budget during active HPO
risk.mitigation: Use consumer GPUs (RTX 3090/4090) for 80%+ of workloads. A100/H100 only for >32 GB VRAM needs. autostop=30min. Dashboard cost tracking (T13).

risk.description: Data sovereignty: MiniVess (EBRAINS, CC BY-NC-SA) on cloud GPUs
risk.mitigation: MiniVess is public data with CC BY-NC-SA license — cloud transfer is permitted. For future restricted datasets, use local GPU or EU-only cloud regions.
